description: Baseline-controlled replication of the bridge-aware premise-retrieval simulation. At matched candidate budget k, compare four cluster-selection rules for cross-domain premise recall on held-out TEST theorems: home-only; bridge(k) = home + top-k neighbour clusters by train-estimated bridge weight W[home][.]; popularity(k) = home + the k globally most-cited clusters (same set for every theorem); random(k) = home + k random clusters, averaged over 20 deterministic seeds. W and the popularity ranking are estimated on the TRAIN split only.
motivation: The original simulation (e-0011) showed bridge-aware retrieval lifts cross-domain recall 61.9%->88.2% at k=3, but lacked a control: maybe adding ANY k clusters helps. This isolates whether the per-home bridge ranking carries real signal beyond a popularity/hub effect or chance.
formal_results: Cross-domain-only mean recall (n=432 test theorems): random(k) barely moves (0.626->0.650 over k=1..10); popularity(k) rises 0.728->0.969; bridge(k) rises 0.740->0.973. bridge beats popularity at every k by a small consistent margin (k=1 +1.22pt, k=2 +0.89pt, k=3 +0.38pt, k=5 +0.62pt, k=10 +0.40pt) and beats random by 11-32pt. Top global-popularity clusters are the few foundational hubs (cluster ids 0,2,1,3,5).
results_description: The bridge graph carries genuine cross-domain signal (it crushes the random baseline), but most of the recall gain over home-only is a global-hub/popularity effect: simply adding the biggest globally-cited clusters recovers ~95-99% of the bridge-aware improvement. Per-home bridge specificity adds only a small (<=1.2pt), though consistently positive, edge over popularity.
conclusions: The bridge-aware retrieval advantage is real but largely explained by global hub popularity, not theorem-specific bridge structure. This tempers a-0013: bridges help premise selection mainly by routing to a handful of integrative hub clusters; the marginal value of the per-home bridge ranking over 'just add the biggest hubs' is small. Honest control that narrows the operational payoff claim for q-0011.
